q: Two compounds I and II are eluted by column chromato-graphy(adsorption of I $>$ II). Which one of the following is a correct statement?
question: Two compounds I and II are eluted by column chromato-graphy(adsorption of I $>$ II). Which one of the following is a correct statement?
options: II moves slower and has higher $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}$ value than I
II moves faster and has higher $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}$ value than I
I moves faster and has higher $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}$ value than II
I moves slower and has higher $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}$ value than I
solution: Since, adsorption of I $>$ II, I is firmly attached to column (stationary phase). Hence, it will move slowly and will move little distance. A1so II is loosely attached to column (stationary phase). Hence, it will move faster and will move large distance.

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of optical isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> – chlorobutane ........
question: Number of optical isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> – chlorobutane ........
solution:
The number of optical isomers possible for a compound is 2n where n= number of asymmetric carbon atoms. 

As n=1 for 2-chlorobutane, it is an optically active molecule with one chiral carbon atom and it forms dextro and laevo isomers.

2n=21=2. 

Hence, it has two optical isomers.

q: The correct IUPAC name of the following compound is
question: The correct IUPAC name of the following compound is
solution:
Aldehydic (-CHO) group has greater priority than keto group and nitro groups is always a prefix.

Root word of the given compound is hept, double bond is the primary suffix and aldehydic group becomes secondary suffix and keto and nitro groups are prefixes.

Hence, the correct IUPAC name of the given compound is

4-Methyl-6-nitro-3-oxohept-4-enal

subject: Chemistry
q:
Shown below is the structure of methyl acetate with three different $\alpha$, $\beta$ and $\gamma$ carbon - oxygen bonds.

The correct order of bond lengths of these bonds is :
question:
Shown below is the structure of methyl acetate with three different $\alpha$, $\beta$ and $\gamma$ carbon - oxygen bonds.

The correct order of bond lengths of these bonds is :
solution:
In methyl acetate, the $\alpha$ bond is a $C=O$ double bond, which is the shortest among the three.

The $\beta$ bond is a $C-O$ single bond adjacent to a carbonyl group. Due to resonance ($CH_3-C(=O)-O-CH_3 \leftrightarrow CH_3-C(-O^-)=O^+-CH_3$), the $\beta$ bond acquires partial double bond character, making it shorter than a pure single bond.

The $\gamma$ bond is a pure $C-O$ single bond between an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon and oxygen, with no resonance involvement. Thus, it is the longest bond.

Therefore, the correct order of bond lengths is $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$.

Answer: $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$

subject: Chemistry
q: Using column chromatography, mixture of two compounds ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ and ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>’ was separated. ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ eluted first, this indicates ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>’ has
question: Using column chromatography, mixture of two compounds ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ and ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>’ was separated. ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ eluted first, this indicates ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>’ has
solution: In chromatography, the retardation factor (Rf) is the fraction of an analyte in the mobile phase of a chromatographic system. In planar chromatography in particular, the retardation factor Rf is defined as the ratio of the distance travelled by the centre of a spot to the distance travelled by the solvent front. The more polar spot travels slower, and the less polar spot travels faster. Rf values, on the other hand, are directly related to the rate of movement. The fastest moving spot has the highest Rf value, least polar (fastest moving), and the spot with the lowest Rf value is the most polar (slowest moving). Hence, the compound B is strongly adsorbed and has low Rf value.

subject: Chemistry
q:
In Dumas' method 292 mg of an organic compound released 50 mL of nitrogen gas $\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)$ at 300 K temperature and 715 mm Hg pressure. The percentage composition of ' N ' in the organic compound is ________ % (Nearest integer)
(Aqueous tension at $300 \mathrm{~K}=15 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$)
question:
In Dumas' method 292 mg of an organic compound released 50 mL of nitrogen gas $\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)$ at 300 K temperature and 715 mm Hg pressure. The percentage composition of ' N ' in the organic compound is ________ % (Nearest integer)
(Aqueous tension at $300 \mathrm{~K}=15 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \cdot \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{RT}} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}=\frac{700}{760} \times \frac{50}{1000} \times \frac{1}{0.0821 \times 300} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}}=2 \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \\ & \text { Mass of } \mathrm{N}=2 \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}} \times 14 \\ & \% \mathrm{~N}=\frac{\text { mass of } \mathrm{N}}{\text { mass of organic compound }} \times 100 \\ & \% \mathrm{~N}=\frac{700}{760} \times \frac{50}{1000} \times \frac{2 \times 14}{0.0821 \times 300} \times \frac{1000}{292} \times 100 \\ & \% \mathrm{~N}=18 \%\end{aligned}$

The percentage of nitrogen in the compound is (Nearest integer)
question: Kjeldahl's method was used for the estimation of nitrogen in an organic compound. The ammonia evolved from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the compound neutralised <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution. The percentage of nitrogen in the compound is (Nearest integer)
solution:
Meq of H2SO4 used by NH3=12.5×1×2=25

% of N in the compound =25×10-3×14×1000.55=63.6

or

Meq. of H2SO4= Meq. of NH3

12.5×1×2=25meq of NH3

=25 millimoles of NH3

So Millimoles of N''=25

Moles of N''=25×10-3

wt. of N=14×25×10-3

% N=14×25×10-30.55×100

=63.66

≈64%

subject: Chemistry
q: Lassaigne's test is used for detection of:
question: Lassaigne's test is used for detection of:
solution:
Lassaigne's test helps to detect Nitrogen, Sulphur, and Halogens in organic compounds. Here, sodium metal Na is heated in the presence of the organic compound inside a fusion tube. The primary objective of this test is that all the available elements are converted by Sodium metal Na into the ionic state. Lassaigne test reaction for sodium is given below:

N+Na+C→NaCN

S+2Na→Na2S

X+Na→NaX

In the third experiment, X can be anything such as Cl, Br or I.

q: Three organic compounds A, B and C were allowed to run in thin layer chromatography using hexane and gave the following result (see figure). The Rf value of the most polar compound is _____ ×10–2
question: Three organic compounds A, B and C were allowed to run in thin layer chromatography using hexane and gave the following result (see figure). The Rf value of the most polar compound is _____ ×10–2
solution:
In thin layer chromatography using hexane, the least polar compound will rise to maximum height and most polar compound will rise to minimum height.

The retardation factor of each spot can be determined by dividing the distance that a particular compound has travelled by the distance between solvent front and initial spotting site. This parameter is dependent on the thin layer chromatographic plate and the solvent.

∴ Rf value for most polar compound

=28=0.25

=25×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following carbanions in the decreasing order of stability.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Arrange the following carbanions in the decreasing order of stability.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Carbanion stability increases with electron-withdrawing groups (stabilize negative charge) and decreases with electron-donating groups (destabilize negative charge).
IV has CHO (strongly electron-withdrawing by inductive effect), most stable.
I has Br (weakly electron-withdrawing).
II is pure benzyl with resonance from ring.
V has CH₃ (weakly electron-donating).
III has OCH₃ (strongly electron-donating, destabilizes the carbanion).
The critical factor is inductive electron-withdrawing ability:
CHO > Br > H > CH₃ > OCH₃.
Therefore: IV > I > II > V > III.

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Basicity is inversely proportional to electronegativity.

Higher the electron affinity or core charge of an atom, the less likely it is to donate its electrons to a proton.
Greater the delocalization of electrons that could potentially donate to a proton, the less able they are to donate.
Greater the electron-withdrawing effects in another part of a molecule, the less likely the electrons on a particular atom are to donate.

subject: Chemistry
q: In sulphur estimation, $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol of an organic compound (X) (molar mass $76$ g mol$^{-1}$) gave $0.4813$ g of barium sulphate (molar mass $233$ g mol$^{-1}$). The percentage of sulphur in the compound (X) is ______ $\times 10^{-1}$ % (Nearest integer)
question: In sulphur estimation, $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol of an organic compound (X) (molar mass $76$ g mol$^{-1}$) gave $0.4813$ g of barium sulphate (molar mass $233$ g mol$^{-1}$). The percentage of sulphur in the compound (X) is ______ $\times 10^{-1}$ % (Nearest integer)
solution:
Mass of the organic compound (X) can be calculated as:

$\text{Mass of X} = \text{Moles} \times \text{Molar mass}$

$\text{Mass of X} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol} \times 76 \text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.152 \text{ g}$

In the Carius method for sulphur estimation, the mass of sulphur in the given mass of $\text{BaSO}_4$ is:

$\text{Mass of S} = \dfrac{32}{233} \times \text{Mass of BaSO}_4$

$\text{Mass of S} = \dfrac{32}{233} \times 0.4813 \text{ g} \approx 0.0661 \text{ g}$

The percentage of sulphur in the organic compound is given by:

$\text{\% of S} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of S}}{\text{Mass of X}} \times 100$

$\text{\% of S} = \dfrac{0.0661}{0.152} \times 100 \approx 43.487 \text{ \%}$

To express this in the requested format:

$43.487 \text{ \%} = 434.87 \times 10^{-1} \text{ \%}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $435$.

Answer: $435$
